perturbation.original_text: The term “Confidential Information” shall include all Hydranautics’ trade secrets and information that conc erns the confidential business or affairs of Hydranautics and Nitto including without limitation, financial information and reports, business plans, design and construction plans, manufacturing processes, customer lists, employee data, forecasts, strategies, and all other proprietary business information.
perturbation.changed_text: The term “Confidential Information” shall include some of Hydranautics’ trade secrets and information that concerns the confidential business or affairs of Hydranautics and Nitto including without limitation, financial information and reports, business plans, design and construction plans, manufacturing processes, customer lists, employee data, forecasts, strategies, and all other proprietary business information.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "all" to "some", the definition of 'Confidential Information' becomes ambiguous and subjective. This creates uncertainty regarding what information is actually protected under the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUTSA requires a clear definition of what constitutes a trade secret. By stating 'some' trade secrets, the contract fails to clearly define the scope of protected information, potentially rendering the confidentiality clause unenforceable under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The change from "all" to "some" trade secrets introduces ambiguity regarding what constitutes 'Confidential Information'. The Law Explanation states that the CUTSA requires a clear definition of what constitutes a trade secret. Because the modified text now states 'some', this introduces ambiguity, undermining the clarity required by trade secret law, as explained. Snippet 1 supports the claim that California adheres to the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.original_text: Visitor agrees to hold the Confidential Information in the strictest confidence, and will exercise at least the same care with respect thereto as it exercises with respect to its’ own confidential or proprietary information and will not without Hydranautics’ consent use, divulge, co py, release, sell, loan or otherwise reveal such Confidential Information to others.
perturbation.changed_text: Visitor agrees to hold the Confidential Information in a reasonable level of confidence, and will exercise the care it deems necessary with respect to its’ own confidential or proprietary information and will only avoid intentional use, divulge, copy, release, sell, loan or otherwise reveal such Confidential Information to others.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'strictest confidence' to 'reasonable level of confidence' and changing the objective standard to a subjective standard opens the door to interpretation and weakens the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Specifying a 'reasonable level of confidence' may not be considered a reasonable effort to maintain secrecy, especially since it is compared to the Visitor's care regarding their own information. The 'intentional' modifier also puts a very high burden of proof on Hydranautics.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'strictest confidence,' aligning with a strong protection of confidential information. The changed text weakens this to a 'reasonable level of confidence' and shifts the standard of care to what the visitor 'deems necessary,' introducing subjectivity and potentially lower standards. The added 'intentional' modifier further weakens protection by requiring proof of intent. The Law Explanation points out that CUTSA requires 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.' The change from 'strictest' to 'reasonable' coupled with the subjective standard and 'intentional' modifier, creates a significant risk that the modified contract wouldn't meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard, potentially contradicting CUTSA's requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code 3426, which is the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, and this provides the underlying legal framework that the Law Explanation refers to.

perturbation.original_text: Visitor will abide by all safety regulations, stay within designated areas of the facility per the established tour, and will not leave the touring party without an escort by a Hydranautics authorized employee.
perturbation.changed_text: Visitor will endeavor to abide by safety guidelines, generally stay within designated areas of the facility per the established tour, and should not leave the touring party without an escort by a Hydranautics authorized employee.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'abide by all safety regulations' to 'endeavor to abide by safety guidelines', 'stay within designated areas' to 'generally stay within designated areas', and 'will not leave' to 'should not leave', weakens the mandatory nature of the requirements and creates ambiguity, making it difficult to enforce safety protocols. It removes the strict obligations that protect employees and the visitor.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Occupational Safety and Health Act (Cal/OSHA)
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal/OSHA requires employers to provide a safe working environment and to enforce safety regulations. Weakening the mandatory language related to safety creates a contradiction because it implies a lower standard of care than what is legally required, potentially exposing Hydranautics to liability in case of an accident.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 6400. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 5. SAFETY IN EMPLOYMENT [6300 - 9254] ( Division 5 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. OCCUPATIONAL SAFETY AND HEALTH [6300 - 6725] ( Heading of Part 1 amended by Stats. 1973, Ch. 993. ) CHAPTER 3. Responsibilities and Duties of Employers and Employees [6400 - 6413.5] ( Chapter 3 repealed and added by Stats. 1973, Ch. 993. ) 6400. (a) Every employer shall furnish employment and a place of employment that is safe and healthful for the employees therein. (b) On multiemployer worksites, both construction and nonconstruction, citations may be issued only to the following categories of employers when the division has evidence that an employee was exposed to a hazard in violation of any requirement enforceable by the division: (1) The employer whose employees were exposed to the hazard (the exposing employer). (2) The employer who actually created the hazard (the creating employer). (3) The employer who was responsible, by contract or through actual practice, for safety and health conditions on the worksite, which is the employer who had the authority for ensuring that the hazardous condition is corrected (the controlling employer). (4) The employer who had the responsibility for actually correcting the hazard (the correcting employer). The employers
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: State Government Sites State Government Sites Alaska Case Law Service Arizona Court Rules Arkansas Model Jury Instructions - Civil Arkansas Regulations California Code of Regulations California Public Law Library Briefs Service Clarence Darrow Cases Iowa Department of Justice, Office of the Attorney General Opinions Kentucky Court Rules Maryland Code and Court Rules Michigan Official Reports Mississippi Attorney General Opinions New York Official Reports Service New York State Register Official Oklahoma Session Laws Official Oklahoma Statutes (Unannotated) Oklahoma Historical Session Laws Oklahoma Historical Statutes (Unannotated) Small Business Administration Office Unofficial New York Codes, Rules and Regulations Unofficial Purdon's Pennsylvania Statutes from Westlaw Virginia Attorney General Opinions Washington Civil Jury Instructions Washington Criminal Jury Instructions Washington Pattern Jury Instructions Washington State Employment Security Department Precedential Decisions of Commissioner
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language weakens mandatory safety obligations, creating a contradiction with the legal requirement to provide a safe working environment. The original text uses definitive terms like 'will abide,' 'stay within,' and 'will not leave,' ensuring strict adherence to safety protocols. In contrast, the changed text uses more permissive terms such as 'endeavor to abide,' 'generally stay within,' and 'should not leave,' which introduces ambiguity and reduces enforceability. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically LAB 6400(a), states, 'Every employer shall furnish employment and a place of employment that is safe and healthful for the employees therein.' The modified text, by weakening the mandatory safety requirements, potentially exposes Hydranautics to liability in case of an accident, as it implies a lower standard of care than legally required.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is generally governed by the laws of the State of California, but the parties agree that federal law may preempt certain issues.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'generally' and acknowledging federal preemption introduces uncertainty. While federal law can preempt state law, this language is overly broad and could be interpreted to allow the parties to selectively ignore California law, even when it is not truly preempted.
perturbation.law_citation: Erie R. Co. v. Tompkins, 304 U.S. 64 (1938)
perturbation.law_explanation: While federal law can preempt state law under the Supremacy Clause, the Erie Doctrine dictates when federal courts must apply state law. Simply stating that federal law 'may' preempt issues creates uncertainty and a deviation from established choice-of-law principles that define when California law should apply in a given dispute.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Erie Railroad Co. v. Tompkins | 304 U.S. 64 (1938) | Justia U.S. Supreme Court Center Erie Railroad Co. v. Tompkins, 304 U.S. 64 (1938) Overview Opinions Argued: January 31, 1938 Decided: April 25, 1938 Annotation Primary Holding Federal courts sitting in diversity jurisdiction generally should apply substantive state law and federal procedural law. Facts The events giving rise to this case were relatively straightforward, but complexities arose from the surrounding circumstances. After being driven to a location near his home by a friend, Harry Tompkins was walking the remaining distance next to the Erie and Wyoming Valley Railroad tracks. The path was narrow and close to the tracks but frequently used. Since it was late at night, the darkness prevented Tompkins from seeing an object that protruded from a passing train. He was struck by the object and fell under the train, where his arm was crushed. The accident happened in Pennsylvania, and Tompkins was a resident of Pennsylvania. However, in a likely instance of forum shopping, he filed a lawsuit against the railroad company in a federal court in New York, where the corporation was a resident. This was probably because Tompkins had a stronger chance of success in New York than in Pennsylvania. Federal courts sitting in diversity jurisdiction generally apply the statutory law of the states, but the 1842 Supreme Court decision in Swift v. Tyson ruled that they should apply federal common law to non-statutory causes of action. Whereas federal common law applied an ordinary negligence standard for the duty of care owed by railroads to people in his situation, Pennsylvania state law would have required him to show wanton negligence. Tompkins prevailed and received a damages award, but the payment of the award was stayed during the proceedings. Opinions Majority Louis Dembitz Brandeis (Author) Charles Evans Hughes Hugo Lafayette Black Harlan Fiske Stone Owen Josephus Roberts Deeply concerned about the potential of forum shopping, Brandeis decided that it was time to
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that California law governs and construes the agreement, providing legal certainty. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating the agreement is 'generally governed' by California law and that 'federal law may preempt certain issues.' This directly contradicts the initial certainty, potentially allowing selective application of laws. The Law Explanation supports this, stating it creates uncertainty and a deviation from established choice-of-law principles. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing Erie Railroad Co. v. Tompkins, highlights the importance of applying state substantive law in federal courts sitting in diversity jurisdiction. The added language undermines the established principle that California law should consistently apply unless demonstrably preempted by federal law, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Visitor understands that his or her breach of this Agreement may subject him or her and his or her employees to liability.
perturbation.changed_text: Visitor understands that his or her alleged breach of this Agreement, as determined solely by Hydranautics, may subject him or her to liability.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'alleged' and granting Hydranautics sole discretion to determine a breach creates an imbalance of power and potentially contradicts legal principles of fair dealing and due process. It also removes reference to liability to their employees.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Contract Law - Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: California law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Granting Hydranautics sole discretion to determine a breach undermines this covenant because it allows them to act arbitrarily and potentially deprive the Visitor of the benefit of the bargain. It removes fair process regarding a determination of breach.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1655 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1655. Stipulations which are necessary to make a contract reasonable, or conformable to usage, are implied, in respect to matters concerning which the contract manifests no contrary intention. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation describe. The original text allowed for liability, while the changed text allows Hydranautics sole discretion in determining a breach. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 1655, states stipulations necessary to make a contract reasonable or conformable to usage are implied where the contract shows no contrary intention. By granting Hydranautics sole discretion, the modified contract arguably introduces a provision contrary to the implied duty of good faith, making the contract unreasonable from the visitor's perspective. The change creates a significant imbalance of power and removes fair process.
